reviews.review_text:
"Capital has the ability to subsume all critiques into itself. Even those who would critique capital end up reinforcing it instead."

It's a cruel irony that this commentary is in the game itself given how horrible the company treated its original writers.

Amazing game, horrible company. Don't give ZA/UM a cent. Stealing a group of people's life work with the loopholes of capitalism isn't a crime by the law, but it's a sure way to get people to boycott anything this company will ever make.

This game and the situation surrounding it truly is like a heartbreak.

It hurts thinking about it. 
It hurts thinking about everything that went wrong.
It hurts thinking about what could have been.
It hurts thinking about how you'll never be able to get something as good, ever... 
Because the world is doomed, because art is dead, because you are doomed, because love is dead, because you don't deserve good things... 

But like any heartbreak, you learn love is still alive not by finding love with someone else, but by finding love withing yourself and even more finding love in the world that surrounds you. Maybe not enough love to fix the world, but enough to make you wake up wanting to see it better, to see yourself better, to see yourself being part of it. Like an incredibly sensitive instrument that receives it, amplifies it and sends it back to it once again.

Dont let this world kill your spirit. You too can make it into the next world, as long as you fight for it. 
Love is alive and love is disco.

Make sure to support the original members future projects. Remember to not buy, find your own ways to experience it. You wont regret it.

An absolutely incredible game that was stolen from the original creators. ZA/UM is now an evil company who is profiting greatly off the game they stole while the original devs who actually made the amazing piece of art that Disco Elysium is aren't seeing a penny and have been royally screwed by ZA/UM

And ZA/UM has the absolute GALL to plaster ads for their rip-off "spiritual successor" game, Zero Parades For Dead ZA/UM Exec-- I mean, Dead Spies,  on the title and pause screens for this game-- forced into your game through this most recent update (an update, which by the way, fixes ABSOLUTELY NOTHING, just forces you to look a their stupid ads without fixing any of the bugs in the game). You can't even roll it back. It's disgusting.

I would recommend playing this game to everyone and anyone-- it is an emotional experience left me a changed person, and it is a unique and incredible game. It may very well be my favorite. However, ZA/UM's behavior has been incredibly shameful with the way they're trying to milk this game for every last cent and their greed is straight-up embarrassing. So, while I recommend playing this game, I would not recommend buying this game from ZA/UM and giving the greedy ****s any more undeserved money, not if you can help it. If nothing else, please don't buy this game at full price haha.

This is a Masterpiece! 
It's art in the best sense: entertaining you with a gripping story and interesting characters, humour and sincerity, hope and hopelessness in an unknown world much like ours - while inviting you to take a stance on a number of social isues, cultural issues, philosophical issues. Making you think, making you laugh, making you feel.

I didn't know what to expect when I started the game, stumbled into it blindly and was instantly sucked in. (Guess I was living under a rock - now I realize it's a pretty well known game. So maybe, when you're going in expecting something grande, it won't have the same effect on you of course. But I think it will still be really good.)
The writing, the voice acting, the graphic style that fits so well in a kinda post-apocalyptic setting - everything is just so well done!

Sometimes the political and geographical world-building was a bit much for me. Even though I admired the creativity, I found myself impatiently clicking faster through some topics because I wanted the story to go on. Of course there is no need to go through all dialogue options, but often it will give you experience points you can use to level up your skills. And sometimes something interesting might pop up - so I clicked through everything available to me even if I was already about to loose patience and wanted to move on. So ultimately I did this to myself and can't really complain. It also still contributed to that world feeling more complete and real.

Honestly I just love this game, go and give it a try!

Corporate greed screws over a group of people who made one of the best written games ever, not very surprising. If you are some kind of superstar you will NOT pirate this game because that would be immoral!

A good super niched game, but please do not buy it anymore. The current co-owner, Tõnis Haavel, was convicted of investment fraud back in 2015. He and his partner siphoned 4.8 million euros directly from the studio's budget through a shell company to illegally buy out the majority shares and kick out the original creators. Buying this game now only lines the pockets of corporate thieves. More about this fraud you can find on Google.

Unmarked spoilers for the entire game ahead. Originally posted on r/DiscoElysium 

I don't remember when I bought Disco Elysium. I know it was after The Final Cut released—probably well after that. Definitely when it was on sale. I had heard how good the game was, how many people considered it their favorite of all time, but did not know any specifics other than it being a narrative RPG. It didn't interest me that much, so like with many games I bought it with the expectation I would play it one day knowing deep down it is likely that day would never come. 

I started the game on March 13th, 2026. I saw that the average playtime was 33 hours, and I was in the mood for a RPG after not having finished one since October that wouldn't take me 50+ hours. I also decided to stream it to some of my friends on Discord.

What followed was 68 hours of what is easily one of the best games I have ever played. I can only think of a handful of games aside from it that showed this much potential in the opening hours, followed through, and actually exceeded my expectations. Every aspect of the game was firing on all cylinders: I couldn't get enough of the writing, the worldbuilding, the characters.

The narration and the voices in Harry's head are the games biggest strength and the number one thing that, if properly described to me, would have made me play it sooner. The way they trip over eachother, all trying to get through to Harry and his broken mind, extending simple conversations into complicated branches of indecision is brilliant. I played with the voice acting on all the time, and the way lines were delivered added to my immersion in a way few other games do (I'm not a VA snob I promise). I did a 3-6-1-3 build, and while I know much of the story wouldn't actually change if I spec'd into other skill points, the way they're delivered to and interpreted by Harry would change. That rocks. This is one of the only games I decided on playing more than once before finishing it because of that. It's also just hilarious. The way the game handles its various political routes (I went for art / commie cop) was a great mixture of playing it straight and making fun of the player, and their were a lot of lines that would be the standout line™ in other games.

Martinaise, along with Revachol and the rest of the described world, is such a well detailed setting I can't help but wish for more even though so much is already explained. The way technology has seemingly stalled despite it potentially being centuries, rather than decades, of years ahead of (or behind) our world. The existence of the pale and it's use as a metaphor for the destruction of the world by human ideals, as well the existence of Innocents could fill a dozen games, while Disco leaves them as background elements for one. The explanations of Dolores Dei in a book and the church comprised some of my favorite moments in the whole game. I'm aware that Disco is based on a setting that existed long before the game ever did,  and you can tell. It's a shame we will almost certainly never see more official works in this world.

I didn't have to adjust to the fact that the game is 99% conversations, since I love talking to different people in real life anyways. Still, I was surprised by how much I was touched by certain conversations. Some of my favorites, in no particular order, are Elizabeth, Jean, Evrart, King Filippe III, Cuno, Cindy, Klaasje , The Smoker and the Sunday Friend, The Dicemaker, Egg Head, Steban, Kim, Soona, The Working Class Woman, Easy Leo, and Joyce. I realize that just listening names is basically pointless but going over each character would take individual posts the length of this one and this is already long enough.

So, sounds like this game is pretty good! BUT IT IS NOT PERFECT. It has one flaw, dare I say something unforgivable; I think having to interact with that one piece of clothing to get into the docks is pretty silly. I missed it for a long ass time and did not find it until someone else pointed it out to me (this only happened one or two other times the entire game). 

So, why am I writing this post? Why is the title an apology? You see, the friends I mentioned earlier have not liked how this play through has gone. I think it was a lot of fun, all things considered, and other than failing to get the body out of the tree for a long ass time (due to my low physique stats) and having most people die in the end (a pretty major ♥♥♥♥-up but hey Kim lived) I'm not sure what went wrong. But these people have played and watched the game before, and several of them described it as the worst playthrough they've ever seen. I am surrounded by haters. So, to make it up to them, I made a promise: I would finish the ending of the game (the tribunal onwards) in five hours flat on May 18th, no breaks. If not, I would have to post here.

I finished the game in... six hours, since I didn't miss the final dream with Dora after walking past the bed numerous times (I had grown a habit of checking every little object throughout the game, but questioned why a bed was needed when the island wouldn't last more than one day). What a sequence. To me, Harry's inability to move on from Dora and his feelings to her are the strongest parts of the game, and its a shame it's extremely easy to miss them. Very few stories, and especially game stories, have touched me that much. 

Disco Elysium is one of the best games I've ever played, and I'll be chasing the way it made me feel for a long time. Sorry about not finishing it an hour earlier. 10/10

"the mask of humanity fall from capital" is  one of the haunting quotes for this game. and also best describe to the downfall of the developers za/um. 

The game that gave birth to new genre disco like. a narrative based RPG. no combat mechanics, purely battle of ideologies, thoughts and conversation.  Conflicts are mostly solved in conversation. Sounds boring but it is not! Unless you don't like reading. 

Heavy in theory and this game doesn't hand hold you! This game really rattled my brain especially with all those french words. i have to open Google every time! 

Since this is the developers first big game, i was willing to overlook all the bugs and inconveniences in the game design and was hoping they will build a better one the second time around 

But the creator was sodomized by the ultras. and we have to rebuilt all over again. Good thing, this game inspired a new gen of game developers , thats why disco like games are starting to show up!  

A thought provoking and inspiring game. There's no game like it. It belongs there at the top of one of the most influential literary work ever. 

F ZA/UM. Play this game on epic if you managed to get this for free or sail the high seas.

This is one of the best videogames I have ever played. Its art-style is incredibly charming and unique and it has got the best writing and world building of any game I have played. It's not overhyped, you should actually try it out for yourself.

Disco Elysium is a masterclass in worldbuilding and game systems transforming what is basically a few streets as well as a handful of really great characters into a maddeningly complete universe and a thoroughly dynamic, powder keg of a story, brought to life via a gorgeous expressionist artstyle along with really well voice acted dialogues.

It might trend a little too much towards absolute nihilism at times and mostly only gives the appearance of choices mattering throughout the adventure but it is still a compelling, thought provoking experience.
One of the best stories I've experienced. I do think the game can start off a bit slow, with the first 2 days but then it just absolutely picks up. Also Kim Kitsuragi.

Close to flawless game, truly worth more than full price to the team that made it.

Since then, 'allegedly' stolen by the publisher and treated entirely without the due respect a work like this has more than earnt.

And now, the trigger for my finally reviewing this superb game: 

A new patch has added a 100MB advert to the main menu for something entirely unrelated to Disco Elysium, a new game from this publisher that I'll never play and will receive a lot of ill will, due or not.  

Even in the best possible case, it would be an action with similarity to epoxy gluing an advert for the Mona Lisa onto the forehead of Michelangelo's David.  You have damaged a masterpiece with your tomfoolery, and it will have also damaged the reputation of whatever thing you, who are of questionable morality, are trying to raise awareness of.

actually one of my favorite games but if I knew the company was so scummy before I'd have sailed the high seas for this. Don't buy this game, don't give your money to ZA/UM (but I highly recommend playing the game <3

It starts off feeling pretty tedious with all the reading and talking you have to do. It can get overwhelming trying to keep track of everyone in the area. After a while though the story starts to pull you in and it becomes one of those games that sticks with you because of how well its written.

There is a lot going on in the game. Talking to people leads to more than you expect and even the smaller tasks give you pieces of the bigger picture. Some of those side quests end up tying back into what you are investigating which makes it worth checking everything. 

The game's RPG mechanics are unique, allowing your own thoughts, skills, and personality traits to shape conversations in unexpected ways. The atmosphere and characters add a lot as well. Not sure everyone will like it though if they want something with more action right away. But if you stick with the dialogue focused mystery it really shows how far a strong story can go on its own.

My favorite part was bullying a locked shipping container in a rhetoric debate.

After its defeat, it opened and brought me face to face with a man so rich that his wealth bent spacetime. I proceeded to scam him out of money, adopt an Ultraliberal hustle mindset, and descend into a schizophrenic episode where I got peer-pressured by my own organs to become a venture capitalist.

10/10. Very disco.

I love this game. The interactions the main character has with himself has got to be the best story telling I've heard from a game in a long time. I love the art and the music that accompanies it. This game is a complete package and it's perfect the way it is.
this game is one of the best i've ever played. don't buy it on steam. don't support ZA/UM.

Infinitely quotable game that would allow you to see the world in the new perspective. I would still remember a random line from it that would made me chuckle years later.
Recommended for all people who are into reading-type RPGs.
beautiful game but dont buy it unless you wanna support a piece of ♥♥♥♥ company

This game took me for a wild ride. Probably the greatest piece of interactive entertainment that has ever been created. Highly recommended. Though there is something sketchy going on with the publishers and original devs, so maybe "acquire it" elsewhere.

there is nothing. only warm, primordial blackness. and the absolute searing agony of your lower lumbar region... the 40-year-old meat sack tried to replace the cargo door struts on the FJ cruiser... and now it is permanently trapped on the living room couch. the limbic system is weeping. three young children are tearing through the house like feral pale-demons. your wife offers you an ice pack and some gluten-free pretzels, but the pain is entirely existential. bought this game because staring at the ceiling was letting the creeping dread set in... just needed something to numb the brain.

didn't expect to have a deep, emotional conversation with my own spinal cord instead. caught myself actually arguing out loud with the television. ellen asked if the pain meds were making me hallucinate. told her no, i'm just doing a full internal audit of my thought cabinet. she groaned so incredibly loud. told her my back wasn't completely broken, my physical instrument check just critically failed on a two-millimeter bolt. she rolled her eyes so hard i thought she was going to vanish straight into the pale. but seriously—playing a cop whose life is a total, apocalyptic disaster made my own broken body feel a lot more manageable. thanks to the devs for making a game that requires zero physical exertion but completely maxes out your volition... real superstar stuff.

[hr][/hr]

[h1]PC Requirements[/h1]
☐ A potato battery wired to a lightbulb
☑ Runs flawlessly on a 1970s ham radio pulling signals from the Pale
☐ Average rig
☐ Fast
☐ Ask Kim Kitsuragi to lend you his notebook

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
☐ Mindless run and gun
☐ Point and click
☑ Dying of a massive heart attack because you tried to gently grab a hideous necktie off a ceiling fan
☐ Arguing with a mailbox about the sociopolitical state of the free market
☐ Punching a foul-mouthed child in the face and immediately having a nervous breakdown

[h1]Grind[/h1]
☐ None at all
☐ Standard leveling
☑ Reading a 1,000-page philosophical manifesto while arguing with your own liver
☐ Collecting empty glass bottles for three hours just to afford a filthy bed for the night
☐ Soul crushing

[h1]Audio[/h1]
☐ Muted
☐ Standard elevator muzak
☑ Your ancient reptilian brain and limbic system screaming at you in a horrific, raspy voice
☐ British Sea Power dropping a soundtrack so beautiful it makes you cry over a cryptid
☐ Pure auditory perfection

[h1]Bugs[/h1]
☐ Flawless
☐ Minor visual glitches
☑ Working perfectly because any glitches are clearly just conceptualizations of your shattered psyche
☐ Failing a 97% chance skill check and blaming the physics engine for your own misery
☐ Held together by duct tape, cheap booze, and sheer electrochemistry
it is not really a game , more like some drunk hobo noir , full of philosophical vibes and very frustrating gameplay, To me its highly overhyped

Being completely honest I was a little bored at first due to how slow the pacing of the game is and it not really giving you a tutorial. I found it much better as time went on though! I highly recommend researching what certain mechanics such as the dices mean as well, definitely helped me stay with the game!
♥♥♥♥ ZA/UM. Will leave a positive review when the game is returned to it's original creators.

Unico defeito desse jogo é que ele acaba.
A arte, world building, os personagens, tudo tem muita alma e dá pra perceber que pensaram muito nos detalhes, exemplo de RPG que mesmo com uma Main Story linear consegue ter muitas peças móveis.
Me lembrou o clássico Vampire The Masquerade.

Logic: "This game has a lot of intrigue and makes interesting meta-commentary. It covers a wide range of topics, and makes hyperbolic but accurate statements. I think it is good."

Encyclopaedia: "As far as I'm aware, barely any of the original names get money from this due to a hostile corporate takeover. You can probably get it 'cheaper' elsewhere.

Conceptualization: "You have to have a very high IQ to understand Disco Elysium."

Inland Empire: "It's a psychedelic kaleidoscope of suffering and beauty!"

Half Light: "What the hell did that last guy say? I don't like big words. Kill him."

Electrochemistry: "Why are you SOBER??? Play it with your favourite drugs or go the ♥♥♥♥ home!"

Shivers: "Remain vigilant. I love you."

Composure: "The game is good and I THINK you can safely recommend it."

Savoir Faire: "Dance. Dance like your life depends on it!"
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. [All this thinking is hard, and you haven't had a drink in 20 minutes. Begin seizing up.]

2. [♥♥♥♥ yourself. VIOLENTLY.]

3. [RESTART COMMUNISM!]

4. "Kim, I think something is deeply wrong with me."

A masterpiece of a game that manages to juggle both style and substance with equal prowess, never sacrificing one for the other, because its style is substance, and its substance oozes with style. This is, hands down, the best writing in any video game, bar none.

Drama: “No, sire. Theatre. A great opera performed atop the corpse of a dying world.”

Effortlessly witty, charming, and elaborate, the writing transforms even the most mundane interactions into something evocative and entertaining, compelling you to click on every trash can and random stranger just to see what the writers come up with next.

Inland Empire: “The trash can knows something about you. Don’t walk away from it, peer into its rancid depths, it wants to tell you a secret written in decay.”

The characters are brought to life through natural performances and believable delivery, ensuring you stay immersed for the entire ride. Some voices may be conveyed better than others, but all are equally great and always match the tone / characters.

Empathy: “Listen to them closely. Every laughter carries exhaustion, every pain beats with the city's dying heart."

Disco Elysium is a CRPG at heart, allowing you to level up your character’s Mind Palace to unlock certain dialogues and thoughts while improving your odds on skill checks. There’s an abundance of different choices and routes you can take, some may not present itself under certain condition and vice versa, making the game highly replayable.

Encyclopedia: “The term ‘CRPG’ originates from ‘computer role-playing game,’ a genre descending from tabletop systems such as Dungeons & Dragons. Though unlike many of its predecessors, Disco Elysium largely abandons combat in favor of psychological and ideological confrontation.”

Failure is rarely fatal, and can sometimes lead to wonderfully bizarre and interesting outcomes.

Half Light: “GOOD. HUMILIATION BUILDS CHARACTER. NOW KICK THAT METAL FURNACE AGAIN.”

This is a game best experienced blind, with save scumming kept to a minimum. You’re not meant to experience everything in a single playthrough, nor does the game expect you to succeed at every skill check.

Volition: “Stand your ground. Accept failure. The game trusts you enough to let you fall apart without punishing you for it.”

It’s an experience, perhaps even art, that I can easily recommend to anyone.

10/10. A once in a decade masterpiece.

ANCIENT REPTILIAN BRAIN:
"Brother... You have found it again... The glowing disco light hidden beneath all that ruin. Its time to wake up and face the music, disco music..."

After all this time, this game needs no new reviews. It's legendary for a reason. If you're looking for a role playing game where the role you play is someone who's burned his whole world down to deal with the pain only to wake up and have to face that pain anyway, you'll want to play this game.

It saddens me to find out about what happened to the original writers of the game and how the company's action completely contradicts the message of the game. I did the mistake of buying the game before finding out what the company has done. This review is solely for the game because, yes, I do think everyone should play this. And to play it by also executing what the game is trying to say? ♥♥♥♥ capitalism, I say pirate it all the way, baby!

What a game. Even after finishing Disco Elysium, I find myself constantly thinking about it. All the characters, stellar voice acting, possible routes/choices and that unique skill tree. Just fantastic. 

Kim Kitsuragi is best girl.
this game genuinely helped with handling my mental illness and despite the grim atmosphere gave me a better outlook on life

10/10 I was horny the whole time I was playing the game, just don't buy it from ZA/UM, find other ways to play it.
Note: don't buy Zero Parades find other ways to play it for free.

Beware that the original developers/story writers of this game aren't with the studio anymore, management absolutely screwed them.

That being said what an amazing game to replay years later, I came into my second playthrough with the goal of not being a sorry cop and man that ♥♥♥♥ doesn't really ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ matter. There was so much more to discover in this game than I did in my first playthrough. I still had a similar build to my first run, just more on the emotional side rather than the logical one. This one of the few games that felt rewarding to hold onto your skill points in order to retry certain choices and story branches. The worldbuilding in this game is absolutely amazing and well thought out. I'm still not done with the game but I have put in way more hours than my first playthrough. I highly recommend everyone to check this game out.

The last truly great videogame ever made. Nothing has compared to it in Gameplay or Artistic value since

Screw you ZA/UM for ensuring we never got a sequel

"Capital has the ability to subsume all critiques into itself. Even those who would critique capital end up reinforcing it instead."

Pirate this masterpiece instead of giving the thief ZA/UM your money.
I will never experience something so beautiful and amazing as this game was. What a fun let down.

Technical clunkiness aside like bugged white checks that are stuck in your journal forever - this game offers the most chaotic and unhinged roleplaying experience I've ever had. The narrative is incredible [spoiler] once you get used to it [/spoiler], providing both deep depression and a grain of idiocy. Being able to unironically scream about your worst political views right into your boss's face or [spoiler] talking to your tie or schizophrenia, perhaps, that can provide you with the case hints [/spoiler] is peak comedy and storytelling. 10/10 for the absolute trash and glory of it.

One of the most surreal, funny, well-written games I have ever played. 

You are a detective that woke up from a really bad hangover with severe memory loss. On top of that, you have different parts of your personality and psyche (ex. your sense of authority, empathy, anger, logic, perception, and many MANY more) whispering in your ear and encouraging you to make different decisions. I told my friend about this game, and he was like, "So is this game like having a D&D party in your head?"

The world is so immersive with detailed lore that keeps the environment full of life. The characters are all fully voice-acted and have interesting personalities and back-stories. The different parts of your mind are also "characters" that are well written. Speaking of writing, there are so many moments that made me laugh out loud.

9.5/10

This is one of the greatest games I've ever played, and in my opinion one of the best pieces of literature I've ever read. Don't buy it - ZA/UM doesn't deserve a penny for something they never created.

Conceptually and Atmospherically a good idea. But the gameplay and overly written lore make it boring. I only finished it in the hopes of it getting better, in search of that Elysium, which never comes, ironically. I feel like I wasted 46 hours of life which should have been entertainment. At least, if I take any positive from it, the writing/mood is inspiring.
Gotta be one of the most politically based games I have ever played. Thank you, Disco Elysium, for stoking my revolutionary optimism.

I started playing this game about two years ago knowing next to nothing about it a few weeks after losing multiple year long friendships with people who at the time I considered family. For the first time I had found a group of people where I felt like I finally truly belonged, only for several years later to have to come to terms with the fact that they now hated me and that I had my own responsibility in things ending this way. From the very first scene this game captivated me and in just three days I hit the end credits.

It was a haunting, miserable yet beautiful experience and words can not describe the way this game touched my at the time broken heart. I had dealt with issues of self-hatred since I was a child and Harry's not only the most beautifully written protagonist I've ever seen in a videogame but also the most awfully realistic depiction I've ever seen of a man whose perception of himself is just so utterly *broken* that he does not consider himself worthy of any kindness or love yet longs for it all the same, and in his suffering misdirects his own rage towards others. 

Even if I don't account for my own personal headspace at the time, every single creative aspect of this game: from the characters, to the worldbuilding, to the visuals, to the music, to the politics is masterfully woven and crafted to perfection. It is a sad, melancholic story and world and yet our characters manage to find meaning and hope in a world where these things may feel futile at times. Multiple times bringing me to tears both from the way it touched me and from the absurd humor sprinkled throughout the narrative.

It saddens me deeply that Disco Elysium is truly lightning in a bottle, the original creators having their world stolen by corporate greed. And even if things were different I truly doubt that we will ever get a game quite like Disco Elysium again. It is an ethereal, magic experience and my biggest regret is that I shall never, ever be able to experience it for the first time again.

However, I am also forever grateful that such a game exists, it managed to make me feel seen in a way no other story had ever done, and it was a huge contributing factor in the fact that as I write this down several years after I last booted it up, although I may not have figured things out yet, I can believe it when I say that there is a reason for all of this.

It is thanks to Disco Elysium that I can finally say:

I am glad to be me. An incredibly sensitive instrument.

Beautiful game but don't give ZA/UM a single dollar. They stole this game from the original creators and fired them, they're a greedy corporation and you should spend your money elsewhere and pirate this.

One of the best stories in gaming, one of the best main characters in fiction, some of the best dialogue and voice acting ever.

♥♥♥♥ ZA/UM, should have just kept on pirating it
Phenomenal voice acting and an absorbing story. Great game. Nice to slow-play what is essentially a choose your own adventure book with brilliant art and actors.

insane.... in a good way.
too much dialogue, but i just skipped through a lot of it anyway

Alcoholic cop wakes up in one of the most well written fictional worlds out there with some of the most memorable characters. Too bad ZA/UM became moralintern wusses and stole the game, so go ahead and pirate it chum👍
(This review was fact-checked by true Revacholian patriots ✔)

This game is pornographically good. 

Unfortunately I only learned about the situation with the devs after putting 20 hrs in this so I can't refund. But to anyone reading this, thinking about buying, fly a flag with skull and bones over your ship instead.

This is a landmark of the industry, and THE devs did great. I'm just gonna use this review to ♥♥♥♥ on the CEO and the shareholders who ♥♥♥♥♥♥ over the OG creators, and how I'm not goning to buy "ZA/UM's" new game.

Disco Elysium is something else. It's not a game. It's more like a book with choices, dice rolls and visuals. I got it because of the hype around it, suffered through the most part because it's slow, frustrating and boring, got intrigued enough to keep going and have now finished it. 
Were those 34 hours worth it? No, for me, they weren't. Most of the time I was bored, just interrupted by a few funny sequences and puzzle solving in my head. Yes, you have so many choices, but I hated that because every dialogue and scene were so dragged out, that it was no fun for me to explore them. Combined with the 'go figure' attitude regarding its systems that just felt like work. 
But I have to admit: the world, the characters, the story are somewhat great and there are tense and emotional moments, that I did not expect to hit so hard. And it is very mature in a subtle way, beyond the bad language, the jokes and the ol' communism vs. capitalism schtick.
Still, I can't flat recommend this. It's like a good book, that is way too drawn out. Try it out, see if you like it. And if not, toss it out.

It's basically a visual novel turned into a point and click game with skill checks.

It's pretty impressive, the game can be very engaging using TTRPG-like mechanics ex. skill checks and of course the writing and script are top tier. (Thankfully they put in customizations like disabling Voice-over. I'd rather read narrative dialog on my own than have it read to me at a slower pace). 

The 24 esoterically-named skills are divided into four primary abilities:
Intelligence
Emotional intelligence
Physical strength
Agility

Everything revolves around those four abilities and the points you put into them at the start of the game, which is permanent so choose wisely. What I found interesting is that succeeding a skill check sometimes doesn't necessarily mean it's a good thing. This means the game plays out somewhat differently based on those scores. 

I can recommend this game if you like classic point and click style games with a strong narrative. The only problem I have with it is there's really no combat like a traditional RPG, it's a point and click adventure game but with skill checks to determine dialog. It's "fun" but just be prepared for a LOT of reading. Unfortunately I might not be finishing this game since it's not really my cup of tea but it's still a thumbs up. 

Edit: Sadly the original crew got screwed over and are no longer with the company and started up their own. So I'll be looking forward to their next game which is called Hopetown. Don't buy this game or at the very least wait till it's very very cheap.
so inventive, depth of character, the creative spark and ingenuity that went into this game. Enormously entertaining

absolutely hilarious game with AMAZING writing, i cant think of a single moment where i wasnt giggling or completely immersed in the incredible streets of Revachol. every mystery felt like a genuine case to solve and roll your sleeves up for while trying to hold in your laughter or lunch. there is so many different ways to solve things or gather information that i doubt anyone could ever actually know all that the game has to offer. 

the only complaint i could possibly think of is not having enough of this absolute peak, i need to have this game injected straight into my veins like Raphael Ambrosius Costeau's finest black tar heroine

This is - finally - a Citizen Kane of videogames. It is absolutely NOT my favorite game ever or the last decade. But it IS a game with breakthrough mechanics (RPG with no combat and psychology instead) and incredible artistic maturity (world building, writing, real world references, atmosphere, characters, story arc and branching, authorship uniqueness). The scandals, the studio splitting, no direct sequel only help to fuel the legend. It is videogames – not movies, not books, not paper RPGs - at their best.

ZA/UM just ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ sucks. Love this game, it's actually amazing. And it isn't those telltale style games, like TWD, DBH, none of that. Your actions have consequences, and even if you fail, you get something out of every failure, and the story is interesting, the characters are complex and well-written.

ZA/UM sucks.
The game is amazing.
End of story.

Perhaps one of the more interesting things about Disco Elysium is that its faults - a lack of pace, a lack of focus - are both characteristics canonical to its protagonist. As if the game is roleplaying itself.

The art is beautiful, the writing sublime, the RPG elements fresh in ways surprising for a genre that we thought had seen it all. You may not love it, but you will respect it.

After playing finish this game, I figured that DIsco Elysium's direction for a Choice Matter game is one of my favourite ones, compared to certain counterparts. DIsregarding the initial start of the game, I was quite impressed by how the story went. I quite dig the characters and the story but I find the ending particularly sightly underwhelming IYKYK.

Highly rated and not overrated is a comment that I can safely make. If you wouldn't mind clicking through the lengthy texts, this is easily one of the most interesting games I have played.
One of the best RPGs ever made. This game is a crazy experience from start to finish. There is no combat, though, which may be a deal breaker for some folks.
I've been enjoying this game so far. I am looking forward to finishing my first run so that I can start another one and see what differences I get.

Just finished the game. I have to say, this has to be genuinely one of the most detailed and "your choices matter" game I've ever played. Not to exaggerate, there is at least a million dialogues in this game depending on which path you choose. Adding on, the scenery was beautiful, and the story was incredibly well written. 

P.S. If you are a new player starting out, press TAB while playing, it will make your gameplay 100% more enjoyable and you will have an idea of what actually to look for and click on.

A ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ masterpiece. I sacrificed my midterm just to finish it lol. Every character is so well written that, countless times i tried to do the "evil route" but i just cant. Especially i just cant fail Kim. The story is so intricately woven that every minute task you thought couldn't be bothered still relates to the case and worldbuilding as a whole. It is so immersive, the artstyle and texts are so complementary I wish this piece of art could hang upon Louvre.
And definitely thought-provoking, beneath the seemingly pessimistic political messages this game gives me so much hope. Actually made me cried. I could write a whole paper analyzing the themes in this game.

" There is nothing you can do about it. You are how you are — and she is how she is. Things will go as they do. Relax. It’ll be okay. This is somewhere to be."

I've postponed writing a Disco Elysium review for a lot of reasons, but after finishing it a couple months ago now and having spent one too many school nights staying up late purely just to finish the game, feeling as if I can't wait any longer due to its impact, the main factor was really because I didn't know how to feel about said impact.

First off, all good things. This has easily made it in the top 10 OAT for reasons I'll go into soon, but when credits began to roll I just felt like I didn't understand enough about what I really, really had just played, especially at like, 4-5 AM in the morning lol. Yes I may have waited a little too long, but I have more of a grasp on the whole experience now than if I had just vomited my thoughts immediately after and, like I said, this is easily a top 10 game of all time if not purely for its writing.

I will say the actual, literal gameplay is very bare bones, point and click to move and interact with your surroundings. Luckily 80% of the time you're engrossed in walls of text boxes reading and listening to some of the best, most in depth as well as entertainingly absurd dialogue/writing you can find, with amazing voice acting in a really, truly unique setting that had me in a chokehold the entire time. 
I had never played the original version of Disco Elysium so I got to experience this with the voice acting, I'm not sure how my experience would have differed but I very, very much enjoyed the entire voice cast as well as every character in the game (just getting it out of the way, the voice actors knocked it out of the park, absolutely stellar job what they were able to accomplish!)

But literally every single character served their purpose to a tee, whether I really did like them or absolutely hated them, each one was given the depth, intrigue, mystery and personality needed for their role in the story. Even outside of the main cast there are 'side' characters, sure, but I put that lightly just because each one still has insane amounts of personality that I really consider them all apart of the cast, which is absolutely stacked. Your main character you play as and Kim obviously the stars of the show just off the top of my head, but I really cant stress enough how insanely well written each of Disco Elysium's characters are, absolute insanity that I was able to grow attached to each funky little character in this world.

This also goes for the world building, the soundtrack, the art style, the setpieces, it is not crazy to say that Disco Elysium may actually be a masterpiece in every sense of the word. My one and only true regret is that I will have never myself come close to being able to truly understand the game when I finished it, let alone capture the vastness of Disco Elysium in one simple review still without having really understood it fully, which it wholeheartedly deserves to be. And is of course what made me so nervous to make this review in the first place. 

There is so much to the world, the political intrigue, the details in character designs/dialogue, the symbolism and meaning of all of it that I simply will have never been able to pick up on or understand in its entirety by myself.  Although I am a JJK fan after all so, my media literacy was doomed from the start ig lol.

I hope I will continue to learn and understand all of these things as this game will sit pleasantly in the back of my mind with the rest of its gnawing questions, and I will gladly answer to them when they so choose to invite me back into its weirdly incredible world once again, 10/10.
really good novel. unfortunately buying this won't support the people who made it happen.

One of the finest stories I've ever experienced. I will not let the controversy behind the company downplay how good of an experience this games is at it's core. Despite the original writers/artists being ousted I will hold onto hope that this game will receive a proper sequel/DLC one day as it was planned (preferably with the original minds behind it, with or without ZA/UM). 10/10, one of those games you only get to play 2-3 times but stays on your mind long after.

Like a lot of people I have been meaning to play this but honestly could not be bothered. The game doesn't give the best first impression in its first couple of hours. Its very slow, clunky, it can be confusing especially if you go in blind and, frankly, it's a terrible detective game as it blocks off lines of enquiry with arbitrary skill checks. But players with more patience than me say this game is great so I finally sat down and forced myself to finish this and discovered a game that is a truly unique RPG, with amazing world building and characters, and a refreshingly on-the-nose discussion of philosophy and politics that, at times, made me reflect on my own beliefs.

The game doesn't take itself too seriously. The humour generally revolves around the main characters crazy antics and nihilistic view of himself. The fact that you can 'game over' by having a nervous breakdown over a mistake is funny (maybe not laugh out loud funny but still funny). For me it felt like the game was encouraging me to be wackier than I wanted. I was playing the role of a detective that just wanted to find the truth and the game called me out as a 'moralist' because I didn't show any particularly interesting political leanings. I felt judged... and I found that funny too.    

Very quickly this game makes it clear that there are a lot of different factors that influence the exact trajectory of the story. Your character's stats, the choices you make, the things you collect, what you wear all impact the nuances of the story and its outcome. This gives the game massive replay-ability but the major story beats seemingly will always be the same (The game is linear).  

Character builds in this game are interesting. For me I wanted a character that was high in intellect (I thought that's good for a detective) but when it came to levelling up I couldn't predict how my stat choices would effect what I would be able to do. The skill checks you come across on your first time playing are always gonna be a surprise and so the game wants you to fail some checks to progress. Something I personally had to overcome playing this is that your build doesn't represent how effective a detective you are, but influences the role you play, so its okay to fail at times that's all part of the whacky adventure you're having. Why then are you given a probability of success on some dialogue checks? I look at a low percentage and think I can't do that so I move on and feel like I'm at a dead end. It meant I wandered around aimlessly sometimes looking for an alternative to the checks I would fail rather than failing said check and actually progress the story.

If you are looking for a game that is rich in world building, thought provoking in story and accessible to play this is perfect. Just don't be worried about failing there is no failure just a different outcomes.

I opened the game for the first time, made a character sheet for a hyper intelligent autistic and nimble freak with horrible people skills and an incredibly frail body. 

Proceeded to jump into a new save. Woke up from a drunken stupor splaid out on my floor in nothing but my underwear. Noticed a tie hanging from the ceiling fan in my room. Tried to grab it. The effort tired my body, gave me a single point of phyisical damage which caused me to suffer a heart attack and ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ die.

Cue the epilogue.

Main menu loads. The "load game" option is greyed out as I died so soon I never even got the chance to save my game.

This game is amazing, brings together table top rpg with an intense story-driven narrative. Everyone should play it at least once in their lifetime. It taught me a lot about the political landscape, and just overall has the best writing in any game I've played so far. The pacing and tension built by the soundtrack makes situations like the tribunal feel so high-action that you'll forget that there isn't actually any combat in this game, in the natural sense. The voice acting is next level, the cast really bring to life their characters. Mutliple endings, and just goofy side quests that genuinely surprise you every time you discover them.
10/10, I hope they make a sequel with Detective Cuno!

Disco Elysium isn’t just a game—it’s a full-blown existential trip wrapped in a detective RPG. It takes everything people love about deep role‑playing and pushes it into territory most games never dare to explore.

What makes it unforgettable is its writing. Every line feels deliberate, sharp, and layered with meaning. The game doesn’t just tell a story; it dissects your character’s mind, your morals, your failures, and your worldview. Your skills aren’t passive stats—they’re voices in your head, arguing, advising, mocking, and tempting you. It’s like having an entire cast living inside your brain. The game never punishes you for leaning into your flaws; it embraces them and turns them into storytelling fuel.

Revachol, the city you’re dropped into, feels alive in a way few game worlds do. It’s bleak, beautiful, broken, and full of people who feel painfully real. Every conversation matters. Every choice has weight. And the themes—politics, trauma, addiction, identity—are handled with honesty and depth.

this game's story is incredible the whole way through, it invokes every emotion in the right way, funny when it needed to, sad, fun, happy. the world building that is better than all the games ive played, had me intrigued the whole way through, rare can a game genuinely awaken this feeling of wonder.

Disco Elysium.. 2019's groundbreaking RPG and winner of seemingly every award for that year, lay dormant on my hard drive for over 5 years.
Not because it's a bad game.. it is because after my initial 30mins of playtime it became evident that a significant portion of my life would need to be set aside to give this game the time that it deserves.
An RPG like no other really.. with some highly original mechanics that work extremely well, allowing for a vast amount of character customisation and experimentation.. all of which affects gameplay.
I love the setting.. a sort of 'dystopic alternate universe'.  The developers invented their own term for this: "Modernopunk". Cool.
The standout here is the incredible writing, intelligent & thought-provoking story and seemingly-endless dialogue.
It's deep, it's dark, it's disco.
9/10

After years of playing the first hour on and off I finally played this game from beginning to end, and it was an experience unlike any other. When you hear "adult game" you think of crappy porn visual novels, but Disco Elysium is what an "adult game" can actually be. Handling topics ranging from race to sex, class consciousness to pretty much every political opinion under the sun. Disco Elysium is truly a one of a kind experience, and by the time you realize you haven't even had a combat section in the game you are taking some heavy and dark turns that you won't see coming. HIGHLY RECOMMENDED.
An extremely captivating story that feels like one of the best crime novels put into video game format.

I don't think I have ever played a game with this much narrative complexity ever, and I doubt I will again. There is so much fully-voice-acted dialogue in this game I legitimately don't even know how you would even begin to undertake such a task logistically. Every single character is so incredibly unique and memorable, and EVERY SINGLE ACTION that you take will affect how the story plays out, directly or indirectly. It's the sort of game where no matter how much you think you've done, there will always be more to do that you hadn't even remotely considered. And honestly, that's the way I like it. A game that tells you how/where/why to do everything is always going to be worse than one that gives you total control over the actions you decide to take, whether you succeed at them or not. And that's in part why this game was just so enjoyable to me. I hope (?) that I got one of the better endings, but even if it wasn't the greatest one (blind playthrough, obviously),  I'm still pretty satisfied with how the final act ended up playing out. 
On another note; the WORLDBUILDING in this game is unlike anything I've seen. So much thought has been put into it, with so much depth in regards to almost every single aspect of it. Every resident of Martinase has their own slice of the world's story to dive into, at virtually every scale of importance, from the great wars of the past to the misguided-yet-promising study of cryptozoology. And ALL OF IT MATTERS. EVERYBODY has something that will help you, you just need to ask the right questions. And that's what the majority of the gameplay loop consists of. You're a detective after all, asking questions is what you do. 
This game is a 10/10. That's not something I give to many games, but it genuinely deserves it. This is a passion project with writing that is most likely gonna stick with me for a good long while. If you have the attention span to, uh, read (yeah I know that's a challenge for a lot of you) ..... then I highly recommend it.

I give it a positive review but only just barely. 
The dialogue, world building, and art direction are all top notch. Literally everything else doesn't hold up. 
The story leans into subverting for subversions sake and the lack of meaningful gameplay sort of drags down the experience. As a walking sim with few meaningful skill checks and very little in the way of meaningful gameplay or strategy to build on it makes the constant backtracking and clicking through menus annoying. 

But again the dialogue and art direction are amazing. 
6/10

Not going to comment on the situation with ZA/UM - just going to review the game.

After one or two attempts before, I finally sat down and got into this game. I think the opening few hours are the hardest for the uninitiated to get through, as you acclimate to the game's pace, style, and quirks. Players will quickly learn that failing is something that will happen often, players who stick around will figure out that failing is meant to be part of the fun. Once you acclimate you'll find a really funny, well written point and click/RPG hybrid that hooks you in by being really unique and unapologetic for what it is. 

By the time I rolled credits on the game I definitely had started growing a little weary of the sometimes incredibly wordy dialogue - much of which is not truly necessary for understanding or appreciating the "main" story (there's a lot of lore that's nice to have but not really pertinent, good mostly for roleplaying if that is your jam). I'm not really advocating for the removal of any dialogue, more just a note that as a player who only dabbles in this genre,  which may be the case for others as well, that it was tough to slow all the way down to the game's pace, especially as you near the climax of the story and you really just want to know the answers and resolve the case. Another thing that exacerbates this issue slightly is the generally slow way you traverse the world - a small issue that snowballs into a larger one over the course of the runtime. Although I wouldn't mind faster traversal in the game, my real recommendation would be just to try to force yourself to SLOW DOWN while playing this game and match its pace. 

Some of the writing was just outside of my ability to truly appreciate what the hell they were talking about, especially some of the inner dialogues the main character has with his "ancient reptilian brain" and "limbic system". Like, I get the big picture, I think, but so much of it just felt like borderline gibberish. But maybe that's the point? And that's why I can't really say if I feel like that's a valid criticism or not, maybe I'm just kinda dumb. I know that I'm bad at reading poetry because it oftentimes feels like I walk away not knowing what was being said, and I felt this way too with some of the more abstract and heady dialogue in this game. Totally willing to admit that some of the topics and dialogue in this game may have just gone over my head and that's not REALLY the game's fault, although I think there is some onus on the writers/developers to write things that the layman will actually understand.

That being said, I LOVE the internal "personalities" that constantly give commentary throughout interactions, sometimes arguing with each other and sometimes giving straight up bad advice or pushing you to do things you shouldn't.

Overall liked the game a lot, not quite the life changing experience some make it out to be, but great nonetheless. Definitely a recommend to people who like CRPGs like Baldur's Gate III, double recommend if your favorite part of BG3 is the dialogue and storylines over the combat. Also a recommend to fans of Fallout, especially New Vegas. Although less action, you will definitely find things to like about the openness of the choices and dialogue this game provides.
